collectives.blurb: Heterogeneous or enumerated multitudes spanning humans, devas, and others — the great dhammābhisamaya audiences at a discourse's close.
A solitary anonymous individual identified only by role, relation, or circumstance (a monk, a brahmin, a laywoman) without a proper name.
Ordained male renunciants of the Buddhist Saṅgha, whether named groups, anonymous bands, or large numerical cohorts of bhikkhū.
Recurring unnamed commentarial archetypes — the discontented monk, the angry monk, the mother-supporting monk, the accused monk — functioning as type-characters.
Celestial beings of the sense-sphere and form-sphere heavens — devatā, devaputtas, accharā, and brahmās — gathered singly or in vast numbers.
Brahmins, matted-hair jaṭilas, tāpasas, wanderers (paribbājakā), seers (isayo) and other non-Buddhist religious practitioners and their pupils.
Householder devotees (upāsakā and upāsikā) and their families who attain while remaining in lay life rather than going forth.
Ordained female renunciants of the Buddhist Saṅgha, including named and anonymous companies of bhikkhuniyo such as Paṭācārā's and Gotamī's followings.
Kings, princes, ministers and their courtly retinues identified primarily by rank rather than by religious community.
Non-celestial non-human beings such as yakkhas and yakkhinīs who do not belong to the deva or brahma heavens.
